source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Question: 22
Two college professors are grading several papers and are required to draw histograms to show the grade distribution at the end of the semester. The test scores are graded on a range of 0 to 100 points. Which of the following claims is correct?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text: The exam results graded by the first professor have a normal distribution because the mean, median, and average are the same.

choice_map.A: The exam results graded by the first professor have a right-skewed distribution because the median is different from the standard deviation.
choice_map.B: The exam results graded by the second professor have a normal distribution because the mode and mean are equal.
choice_map.C: The exam results graded by the first professor have a normal distribution because the mean, median, and average are the same.
choice_map.D: The exam results for the first professor cannot be visually represented because two of the students achieved all of the points available.
choice_map.E: The exam results for the second professor have a right-skewed distribution because the mode and mean are larger than the median.

extraction_notes: answer field is a numeric index/stringable number; unable to determine with certainty whether it is zero-based or one-based, so kept raw answer as-is
image field contains an in-memory PIL image representation rather than a path/URL/filename, so no image path was extracted
source_intake_record.subject: Math

issues: metadata.extraction_notes states the original image field was an in-memory PIL image representation rather than a path/URL/filename.

image_dependency: requires_image=true and text_dominant=false indicate the item depends primarily on visual content.
The question explicitly refers to histograms showing two professors' grade distributions, which are not described in the text.
asset_registry_record.image_manifest shows 1 available image, so the needed visual artifact is present.
multi_step: Solving requires reading the histogram shapes for at least two professors and relating those shapes to distribution claims.
The choices require combining visual interpretation with statistical concepts such as normal vs. right-skewed and mean/median/mode relationships.
There are 5 answer choices, so the solver must evaluate competing claims rather than extract a single visible fact.
verifiability: The response is effectively multiple-choice: the provided answer text corresponds to option C, making correctness checkable against the listed choices.
The visual asset is present and registry_passed=true, so a reviewer can compare the answer against the histogram image.
The claimed rationale uses standard, rule-like statistical properties, which supports downstream checking.

risk_flags: Image height is only 70 pixels, which may limit readability despite registry_passed=true.
metadata.extraction_notes states the original image field was an in-memory PIL image representation rather than a path/URL/filename.

decision_reasons: The sample retains core recoverable semantics: question text, answer text, and a primary image artifact are all present.
The item is highly image-dependent, and the available image is only 70 pixels tall, creating a meaningful readability risk.
There are asset/metadata inconsistencies, including empty metadata.image_paths and an original image noted as an in-memory PIL representation.
The answer typing is inconsistent across records, which adds normalization risk.
Because the core assets still exist, this should not be rejected, but the quality risks make it lower priority than cleaner samples.
